title: From water to the boundless
summary: Anaximander accepted Thales's question — what is the source of everything? — but rejected his answer. Any definite element would unfairly constrain the others; the source must be itself indefinite, the apeiron.

title: Back to a definite element
summary: Anaximenes returned to a tangible substance — air — arguing that change through condensation and rarefaction explains the world without needing the obscure apeiron.

title: If everything flows, nothing is
summary: Parmenides set himself against the Heraclitean image of universal flux: if a thing changes, it both is and is not, which is contradictory. Reality, properly thought, must be one and unchanging.

title: Paradoxes in the service of Eleatic Being
summary: Zeno did not argue for motion's impossibility on its own behalf; he showed that common-sense pluralism leads to absurdity, indirectly vindicating Parmenides's claim that being is one.

title: Saving Parmenides with four roots
summary: Empedocles preserved the Parmenidean rule that being neither comes from nothing nor passes into nothing by making the elements eternal. What looks like change is really the mixing of unchanging roots.

title: Heraclitean tension formalized
summary: The Heraclitean intuition that opposites animate the world reappears in Empedocles as two named cosmic forces — Love and Strife — alternately gathering and scattering the elements.

title: Everything contains everything
summary: Where Empedocles allowed only four elements, Anaxagoras argued that every part of the world contains seeds of every quality; difference is a matter of which seeds predominate. And above the mixture: Mind.

title: Many beings in the void
summary: Democritus accepted Parmenides's principle but multiplied it: each atom is an Eleatic being, indivisible and unchanging. Motion becomes possible because there is also non-being — empty space — between them.

title: If all flows, man becomes the measure
summary: In a Heraclitean world without fixed natures, truth has nowhere stable to stand. Protagoras drew the consequence: what is true is what appears true to each perceiver.

title: Nothing exists, and if it did we could not say so
summary: Gorgias parodied Eleatic argument by deriving from its own style the conclusion that nothing is, that nothing could be known, and that nothing could be communicated. Logic turned against ontology.

title: Plato as Socrates's voice
summary: Plato preserved and transformed Socrates's relentless questioning into written dialogue. The historical line between teacher and pupil is invisible because Plato made it so: Socrates speaks for both.

title: Pythagorean number behind Platonic Form
summary: Plato inherited from the Pythagoreans the conviction that mathematical order is more real than the sensible world, and that the soul, akin to it, can recollect that order.

title: Eleatic Being plural and intelligible
summary: Plato's Forms are Parmenidean Being divided: each Form unchanging and eternal, yet many Forms exist. The dialogue Parmenides has the old man challenge that very move.

title: Heraclitus pushed into appearance
summary: Plato accepted Heraclitus — about the sensible world. Below the moon everything flows; above it, eternal Forms stand still. Flux became a feature of mere appearance.

title: Against the man-measure
summary: Plato's Theaetetus dismantled Protagoras's relativism: if all opinions are true, the opinion that Protagoras is wrong is also true. The doctrine refutes itself.

title: Aristotle against the world apart of the Ideas
summary: Aristotle objected that separating Forms from things explains nothing and only multiplies what needs explaining. He brought form back into the concrete object as its way of being organized.

quotes.text: To say that the Forms are paradigms and that other things participate in them is to use empty words and poetic metaphors.

title: Against the void and the atom
summary: Aristotle praised Democritus's argumentative care but rejected indivisible bodies and empty space. Nature for him is plenum and continuous; atomism cannot explain qualitative change.

title: Praise and reproach for Anaxagoras
summary: Aristotle commended Anaxagoras for introducing Mind into nature but blamed him for using it only as a deus ex machina when natural explanation failed. A first cause should do real work.

title: The four causes against four elements
summary: Aristotle absorbed the four roots into his theory of material cause but criticized Empedocles for confusing the role of love and strife. Causes, he argued, must be classified, not personified.

title: Socrates gone mad
summary: Antisthenes, Diogenes's master, came from Socrates. Diogenes took the Socratic injunction to examine one's life and pushed it into public scandal: live as if convention is a lie that needs exposing.

title: Atomism in the service of tranquility
summary: Epicurus took over Democritean atomism and added a small but crucial swerve. The point was no longer cosmology for its own sake but a physics that made fear of gods and afterlife groundless.

title: Against teleology
summary: Epicurus rejected Aristotle's teleological cosmos in which everything tends toward a natural end. There are only atoms, void, and the contingent arrangements they happen to produce.

title: The poem of the atom
summary: Lucretius did not so much develop Epicurean doctrine as transfigure it. De rerum natura argues by image as much as by inference, carrying the master's atomism through six books of Latin verse.

title: Heraclitean logos becomes Stoic providence
summary: Stoic physics took the Heraclitean logos — the hidden rational pattern of the world — and made it the divine reason that orders every event. The wise life is alignment with it.

title: Stoicism after the Socratic question
summary: The Stoics inherited from Socrates the claim that virtue is the only true good and that knowledge of the good entails its practice. They built a complete system on what Socrates had merely insisted on.

title: From Cynic shamelessness to Stoic discipline
summary: Zeno studied with Crates, a Cynic in Diogenes's line. The Stoic ideal kept the Cynic conviction that conventions are arbitrary, but tempered it into a sober ethics of duty.

title: Stoicism for those who suffer
summary: Epictetus distilled Zeno's system into a handbook: distinguish what depends on you from what does not, and the rest of ethics follows. A slave's philosophy that emperors quoted.

title: An emperor reads a slave
summary: Marcus Aurelius kept Epictetus's Discourses by his side. His Meditations are a daily attempt to apply what Epictetus taught about the dichotomy of control to the unique exposure of imperial life.

title: Seneca's bedside Stoicism
summary: Seneca translated Stoic ethics into the rhythms of Latin letters: how to lose a friend, manage anger, prepare for death. The system became personal correspondence.

title: Systematizing Pyrrhonian doubt
summary: Pyrrho left no writings. Sextus codified the skeptic's practice into modes — recurring strategies for showing that arguments balance on every side and that tranquility lies in not concluding.

title: Plato in a Roman key
summary: Cicero modeled his political and philosophical dialogues on Plato while filtering them through Roman public life. The image of philosophy as conversation among gentlemen is his.

title: Plato as mystical metaphysics
summary: Plotinus read Plato as already proposing a hierarchy of reality descending from a single source. Above even the Forms stands the One, ineffable; the philosopher's life is the journey back to it.

title: Aristotelian Nous absorbed
summary: Plotinus folded Aristotle's self-thinking Intellect into his system as the second hypostasis, below the One. Aristotle is preserved by being placed under Plato.

title: Neoplatonism baptized
summary: Augustine read Plotinus before he read Paul and never lost the Neoplatonic frame: a single source, a fall into multiplicity, a return through interiority. Christianity then gave him the events of that return.

title: Platonic Forms as divine ideas
summary: Augustine moved Plato's Forms into the mind of God. They are no longer a separate realm but the eternal patterns by which God knows and creates. Christianity inherits Platonism by reinterpreting its address.

title: Cicero's Hortensius and conversion
summary: Augustine credited a lost dialogue of Cicero — the Hortensius — with turning him toward philosophy as a way of life. The pagan rhetorician taught the future bishop how to want wisdom.

title: Carrying Aristotle into the Middle Ages
summary: Boethius translated and commented on Aristotle's logical works. For seven centuries, Latin Europe knew Aristotle through him alone; the medieval university begins with that legacy.

title: Augustinian method, scholastic precision
summary: Anselm took the Augustinian motto — believe so that you may understand — and applied it with new logical rigor. Faith provides the matter; reason, working from inside it, can sometimes prove what it already trusted.

title: Sharpening reason in service of faith
summary: Abelard followed Anselm in giving reason a leading role in theology, but sharpened the method until even sacred authorities could be played dialectically against one another in Sic et Non.

title: Aristotle distilled and extended
summary: Avicenna read Aristotle with Neoplatonic eyes and added what Aristotle had not made explicit: a distinction between what a thing is and that it is. Existence enters the metaphysical vocabulary.

title: A chain of intelligences from the One
summary: Avicenna's cosmology descends in a Plotinian arc: from the Necessary Existence to a series of intelligences, each generating the next. Islamic theology and Greek mysticism meet.

title: The Commentator
summary: Averroes stripped Aristotle of the Plotinian elements added by Avicenna and tried to recover the philosopher more or less as he was. The Latins called him simply The Commentator.

title: Against Avicennian additions
summary: Averroes rejected the real distinction between essence and existence that Avicenna had drawn. Existence, he argued, is not an extra ingredient added to essence; it is the actual essence itself.

title: Aristotle inside Jewish tradition
summary: Maimonides held that the truths of philosophy and of Torah cannot conflict. Where they appear to, the text must be read figuratively. Aristotle thus enters Jewish theology as its sober counterweight.

title: What we cannot say of God
summary: Maimonides extended Avicenna's caution about predicating attributes of God into a full negative theology. To say God is good is really to deny that God is not-good; positive attributes mislead.

title: The Philosopher inside the Summa
summary: Aquinas absorbed Aristotle so completely that he calls him simply 'the Philosopher.' Aristotelian metaphysics, ethics, and politics become the natural floor of a building completed by revelation.

title: Augustinian grace, Aristotelian nature
summary: Aquinas remained Augustinian on grace, sin, and the inner life while turning to Aristotle for the workings of nature. The two emperors of Christian thought are arranged into floors of one structure.

title: Against the single intellect
summary: Aquinas rejected Averroes's thesis that there is one intellect for all human beings. Without an individual intellect, the soul cannot be the form of this particular body and personal immortality collapses.

title: Existence as act, not accident
summary: Aquinas accepted Avicenna's distinction between essence and existence but refused to make existence an accident superadded to essence. Existence is the very act by which an essence is real.

title: Against the ontological argument
summary: Aquinas rejected Anselm's attempt to derive God's existence from the concept of God alone. We do not know the divine essence well enough to pull existence out of it; proofs must start from observed effects.

title: Being said univocally of God and creature
summary: Where Aquinas spoke of being analogically, Scotus insisted that the word must mean the same thing of God and of finite things, or theology cannot get off the ground.

title: Cutting metaphysics down to size
summary: Ockham accepted nothing in reality but individuals. Universals are only signs in the mind; the elaborate metaphysical apparatus Aquinas erected over essences and substantial forms simply has nothing to do.

title: Against the haecceity
summary: Scotus added a 'thisness' that individuates each thing. Ockham removed it: individuals are already individual without needing any extra metaphysical glue.

title: Politics without the good life
summary: Aristotle made politics a branch of ethics, aiming at the good life. Machiavelli severed the link: a successful ruler studies what people are, not what they should be, and acts accordingly.

title: Ancient skepticism in the essay
summary: Montaigne read the Outlines of Pyrrhonism and adopted the skeptic's restraint, but turned it inward. 'What do I know?' becomes not a debate position but a way of writing about oneself.

title: Letter and essay
summary: From Seneca, Montaigne inherited the idea that philosophy belongs in letters about ordinary matters. The essay grows out of the Stoic epistle and absorbs its informality.

title: From the One to infinite worlds
summary: Bruno turned Plotinian emanation into a cosmological program: if the source is infinite, why should it generate only one world? The God of Plotinus becomes immanent in an infinite universe.

title: A new Organon
summary: Bacon attacked the Aristotelian syllogism for assuming what should be discovered. Real knowledge requires patient induction from carefully arranged observations, not deduction from received premises.

title: Against the political animal
summary: Aristotle held that human beings are by nature political. Hobbes denied it: we associate from fear, not affinity. The body politic is engineered, not organic.

title: From Baconian method to political mechanics
summary: Hobbes worked as Bacon's secretary in his youth. He inherited the conviction that nature could be analyzed mechanically and extended the program to the body, the mind, and the state.

title: Realpolitik with a theory
summary: Machiavelli looked unblinkingly at how power works. Hobbes added a metaphysical foundation: matter in motion, fear of death, contract. Realism becomes a system.

title: Sweeping aside the scholastic curriculum
summary: Descartes was educated on Aristotle by the Jesuits and spent his philosophical career trying to start over. The Meditations are written as if no textbook had ever existed.

title: An Augustinian cogito
summary: Augustine had already written that even if I am deceived, I am. Descartes sharpened that thought into the central pillar of modern philosophy and acknowledged the resemblance reluctantly.

title: Doubt turned into method
summary: Montaigne lived a literary skepticism. Descartes converted the same instrument into a systematic procedure: doubt everything once, then build back only what survives.

title: Cartesianism through Augustinian eyes
summary: Malebranche took Descartes's dualism with full seriousness: mind and body share nothing, so they cannot truly interact. Only God, occasioning each event, can connect them.

title: Spinoza completes Descartes against himself
summary: If a substance is what depends on nothing else, then by Descartes's own definition only God qualifies. Spinoza drew the consequence Descartes refused: there is only one substance, and we are all modes of it.

title: Jewish rationalism radicalized
summary: Spinoza took Maimonidean caution about scriptural anthropomorphism and pushed it past its theological frame. The Bible is read as a human document; God is identified with nature.

title: Power and right coextensive
summary: Spinoza adopted Hobbes's identification of natural right with natural power but used it to argue for democracy rather than absolute monarchy: the same fact about human beings can ground very different states.

title: Many substances, not one
summary: Leibniz feared the moral consequences of Spinoza's monism. His monads preserve real individuals — each a complete point of view — by multiplying substances rather than collapsing them into one.

title: Against the geometrical body
summary: Leibniz rejected Cartesian extension as the essence of matter. A body conceived as pure geometry has no internal principle of activity, and explains neither motion nor unity.

title: Aristotelian forms updated
summary: Where most early moderns abandoned substantial forms as scholastic clutter, Leibniz recovered them as something like internal principles of action — the seeds of his monadology.

title: Against the inborn mind
summary: Locke directed his Essay against the Cartesian claim that the mind comes furnished with innate concepts of God, self, and the simple natures. Show me a child, he replied, who has them.

title: Limited government against the Leviathan
summary: Where Hobbes saw the state of nature as war, Locke saw it as inconvenient but governable by reason. The sovereign is therefore limited, accountable, and revocable.

title: Locke without matter
summary: Locke had argued that we know only ideas, while still believing in a material substance behind them. Berkeley pointed out that the material substance does no work — and removed it.

title: Idealism via occasionalism
summary: Malebranche held that we see all things in God. Berkeley converted that intuition into an idealism: if every idea must be in some mind, and the world is ideas, then the world is in God's mind.

bridge: Empiricism without matter and without mind
title: From immaterialism to bundle theory
summary: Berkeley dispensed with matter while keeping spiritual substances. Hume turned the same instrument on the soul: looking inward I find perceptions, never the perceiver. The self joins matter on the discard pile.

title: Empiricism inherited, sharpened
summary: Hume took over Locke's program — all ideas traced to experience — and applied it more rigorously. Notions Locke had let pass (causation, substance, the self) failed the test.

title: A modern Pyrrhonism with manners
summary: Hume admired ancient skepticism but found it unlivable in its pure form. His mitigated skepticism keeps Pyrrhonian doubt about metaphysics while accepting that custom, in practice, runs our lives.

title: Separation of powers extended
summary: Montesquieu took the Lockean idea of dividing political power and worked it out into a comparative theory: how laws differ by climate, history, and constitutional design.

title: Candide against the optimism
summary: Voltaire ridiculed Leibniz's claim that this world is the best God could choose by sending Candide through every imaginable catastrophe. The argument is half novel, half tract.

title: Locke for the French public
summary: Voltaire's Philosophical Letters introduced Locke to French readers as a model of empiricist sobriety and religious toleration, against the rationalism and dogmatism he saw at home.

title: The state of nature reconsidered
summary: Hobbes painted natural man as solitary, poor, and at war. Rousseau replied that the picture is really civilized man retrojected. Original humans were peaceable; society made them what Hobbes described.

title: Property as original sin
summary: Locke grounded property in labor. Rousseau countered that the first fence around a field was the origin of inequality, war, and the corruption of natural freedom.

title: Against Sophie
summary: Rousseau had argued in Emile that boys should be educated for reason and girls for charm. Wollstonecraft used Enlightenment premises against him: if reason is universal, the education of girls is a scandal.

title: Moral sentiments after Hume
summary: Smith built his moral theory on Humean foundations: judgment is rooted in sympathy, not deduction. The impartial spectator is Hume's framework refined.

title: The Baconian project as Encyclopédie
summary: Bacon dreamed of an organized inventory of human knowledge in the service of practical progress. Diderot edited it: the Encyclopédie is Baconianism realized in twenty-eight folio volumes.

title: Humanism against the schools
summary: Erasmus mocked the elaborate distinctions of late scholasticism. The Christianity worth keeping, he insisted, is closer to the Sermon on the Mount than to the Summa.

title: Kant and the awakening from dogmatic slumber
summary: Hume had argued that the empiricist program leaves causation, substance, and the self without rational foundation. Kant accepted the diagnosis and answered by relocating those structures: not in the world but in the categories the mind brings to it.

quotes.text: I freely admit that it was the recollection of David Hume which, many years ago, first interrupted my dogmatic slumber.

title: Against rationalist overreach
summary: Kant grew up on Leibnizian-Wolffian textbooks and turned against them. Pure reason cannot deliver knowledge of substances, monads, or a pre-established harmony; it can know only the forms of possible experience.

title: Rousseau's effect on the categorical imperative
summary: Kant said Rousseau taught him to respect the common man. The categorical imperative is the formal version of Rousseau's intuition that no one — peasant or king — may be treated merely as a means.

title: Removing the noumenon
summary: Fichte argued that the unknowable thing in itself was the part of Kant's system that could not survive Kant's own argument. He removed it. Reality becomes the self-positing activity of the I.

title: Putting nature back
summary: Schelling complained that Fichte had reduced everything to the I. Nature is not just the I's other; it has its own development, and the Absolute is the indifference point at which nature and spirit coincide.

title: Spinozism restored
summary: Schelling rehabilitated Spinoza for German Idealism, treating the single substance as the deep truth that idealism had to absorb rather than abandon.

title: Hegel and the limit that will not stand
summary: Kant marked the boundary between phenomena and the unknowable noumenon. Hegel argued that to draw such a boundary is already to think across it, and so the boundary undoes itself.

quotes.text: To draw a limit presupposes that one is, in some sense, already beyond it.
quotes.source: Paraphrase of an argument in the Science of Logic

title: Against the night in which all cows are black
summary: Hegel mocked Schelling's Absolute as a featureless identity into which all differences vanish. Spirit cannot be the indifference of opposites; it must work through them in time.

title: Aristotelian Nous reborn
summary: Hegel ended his system with a citation from Aristotle's Metaphysics. Absolute Spirit, like the Prime Mover, is thought thinking itself — but now also the historical labor by which it gets there.

title: Spinoza, but as subject
summary: Hegel praised Spinoza but found his substance dead — pure being without motion. The cure is to think substance as subject: as the self-developing process he calls Spirit.

title: There is nothing of Heraclitus I have not adopted
summary: Hegel acknowledged Heraclitus as the first to think becoming as the truth of being. The dialectic is Heraclitean tension formalized into a logic.

title: The noumenon named
summary: Where Kant left the thing in itself blank, Schopenhauer identified it: it is Will. The world we know as representation is the appearance of a blind, restless striving that we feel in ourselves as desire.

title: Platonic Ideas as objectifications of Will
summary: Schopenhauer reintroduced Plato's Ideas as the stable patterns through which the Will objectifies itself. Art lets us contemplate them and, for a moment, escape striving.

title: Against the cunning of reason
summary: Schopenhauer regarded Hegel as a charlatan whose dialectic served as flattery to the Prussian state. History is not Spirit unfolding; it is the same Will producing the same suffering on different stages.

title: God is the projected human
summary: Hegel had treated religion as a stage on Spirit's path to self-knowledge. Feuerbach turned the genealogy around: the divine attributes are human attributes projected into infinity. Theology is unreflected anthropology.

title: Against the system
summary: Kierkegaard mocked Hegel's system for forgetting the one thing that cannot be systematized: the existing individual. Truth, for someone with a life to live, is subjectivity — not what is universally provable but what one can commit to.

title: Socratic irony at the door of faith
summary: Kierkegaard wrote his dissertation on Socratic irony and adopted the strategy: pseudonyms, indirect questioning, refusal to instruct. The reader is led to her own existential decision, not lectured.

title: Putting Hegel back on his feet
summary: Marx kept Hegel's dialectic and threw away his idealism. The motor of history is not Spirit but material production; class struggle, not the unfolding of the Idea, drives the transformations Hegel had narrated.

title: Materialism that interprets vs. one that changes
summary: Marx accepted Feuerbach's reduction of theology to anthropology but found it too contemplative. Philosophers have only interpreted the world; the point is to change it.

title: Reading the classics against themselves
summary: Marx took Smith's labor theory of value seriously enough to use it against the system Smith helped legitimize. What looks like voluntary exchange conceals the systematic extraction of surplus.

title: The shared work
summary: Engels financed Marx's life and after his death edited the unfinished Capital. The later doctrine called Marxism is in large part Engels's systematization of work the two had done together.

title: Nietzsche: how the true world finally became a fable
summary: Plato divided reality into a changing sensible world and a true world of Forms. Nietzsche dedicated his work to dismantling that division — not by proposing a third world but by showing the true world was always a symptom of weakness.

quotes.text: Christianity is Platonism for the people.
quotes.source: Nietzsche, Beyond Good and Evil, Preface

title: Will yes, denial no
summary: Nietzsche read Schopenhauer in awe and then turned against him. He kept the Will but refused the conclusion that we should escape it; the wise response to striving is not denial but affirmation, even of eternal return.

title: Socrates as symptom
summary: Nietzsche read Socrates as the first decadent: a Greek who could no longer trust instinct and put dialectic in its place. Reason becomes a tyranny for those who have lost their footing.

title: The categorical imperative as Christianity in disguise
summary: Nietzsche heard Kant's categorical imperative as Protestant duty rewritten in transcendental syntax. The universal moral law is the Christian conscience reinstalled where God once stood.

title: Heraclitean affirmation
summary: Nietzsche found in Heraclitus a thinker who affirmed becoming without consoling himself with a stable world behind it. The doctrine of eternal recurrence is Heraclitean fire taken seriously.

title: Sentiment turned into calculation
summary: Hume had grounded morals in feeling, especially the pleasure of approval. Bentham took the next step: if pleasure and pain are what matter, then ethics is the calculus of summing them.

title: Bentham humanized
summary: Mill kept Bentham's framework but refused to count pushpin and poetry equally. Some pleasures, those engaging higher faculties, count for more — a move both saving and complicating utilitarianism.

title: Against the empty law
summary: Mill complained that Kant's categorical imperative, when actually applied, smuggles in consequentialist reasoning. The supposedly pure principle either makes no demands or makes utilitarian ones in disguise.

title: The careful inheritor
summary: Sidgwick took Mill's utilitarianism and submitted it to merciless examination. He preserved the doctrine but exposed the dualism of practical reason — egoism and utilitarianism — that it could not finally reconcile.

title: Kantian categories made semiotic
summary: Peirce read Kant carefully and replaced his table of categories with a triadic system of his own: firstness, secondness, thirdness. The transcendental conditions of experience become the conditions of any sign.

title: Peirce popularized — and changed
summary: James borrowed Peirce's name and method but loosened them. Truth for James is what works in the widest sense, including the will to believe. Peirce, distressed, renamed his own doctrine pragmaticism.

title: Pragmatism into politics and education
summary: Dewey extended James's pragmatism out of the philosophy of mind into schools, courts, and political life. Inquiry is collective problem-solving, and democracy is the form that maximizes it.

title: Hegel without the Absolute
summary: Dewey began as a Hegelian and never quite stopped. He kept the idea of mind as embedded in communal practice and of inquiry as developmental, while dropping the metaphysics of Spirit.

title: Phenomenology from Brentano's lecture hall
summary: Husserl studied with Brentano and inherited the doctrine that consciousness is always consciousness of something. He turned that descriptive psychology into a more ambitious science of pure structures of experience.

title: Phenomenology as Cartesian
summary: Husserl titled one of his major books Cartesian Meditations. The phenomenological reduction is a methodical doubt: bracket the world and attend to consciousness as it is given to itself.

title: Kantian conditions, phenomenological descriptions
summary: Husserl borrowed Kant's transcendental orientation but refused his deduction-from-above. Instead of inferring categories, the phenomenologist describes the actual structures by which objects show up.

title: A Spinozism of duration
summary: Bergson's metaphysics of duration shares with Spinoza an immanent God-or-Nature that expresses itself in everything. But where Spinoza thought eternity, Bergson thinks the flow of time.

title: Time spatialized vs. duration lived
summary: Kant treated time as a form of inner sense, in effect homogenizing it. Bergson protested that the time we live is heterogeneous flow — duration — and that science's clock-time is its spatialized double.

title: From consciousness to existence
summary: Heidegger took phenomenology from his teacher Husserl and turned it from a science of consciousness into an inquiry into the meaning of being. Husserl never forgave him for it.

title: Aristotle read against scholasticism
summary: Heidegger spent years reading Aristotle in Greek without the medieval categories in the way. The result is a strange, original Aristotle whose questions about being become Heidegger's own.

title: Anxiety becomes ontological
summary: Anxiety, conscience, and being-toward-death come into Being and Time from Kierkegaard. Heidegger generalized them: what Kierkegaard described in religious terms reappears as structures of Dasein.

title: Nietzsche read as the last metaphysician
summary: In his lectures of the 1930s, Heidegger argued that Nietzsche, far from overcoming metaphysics, completed it. The will to power and the eternal return are the final form of Western thinking about being.

title: The Heideggerian critique as diagnosis of Descartes
summary: Heidegger read Descartes not as a thinker to be refuted but as a symptom: the moment when the West began to treat being as objectivity, the world as picture, and nature as standing reserve for a sovereign subject.

quotes.text: ...to render ourselves the masters and possessors of nature.
quotes.source: Descartes, Discourse on the Method, Part VI

title: Kant as thinker of finite transcendence
summary: In Kant and the Problem of Metaphysics, Heidegger read the Critique not as epistemology but as a buried ontology: the transcendental imagination is the finite human opening onto being.

title: Kierkegaardian limit situations
summary: Jaspers took Kierkegaard's analyses of anxiety, despair, and faith and reframed them as limit situations — death, guilt, conflict, suffering — where the self either retreats or becomes itself.

title: Heideggerian Dasein becomes free consciousness
summary: Sartre read Being and Time as a philosophy of freedom and built Being and Nothingness on a French reinterpretation: Dasein is the for-itself, an empty consciousness that must define itself by what it does.

title: Phenomenology in French
summary: Sartre encountered Husserl in Berlin and came back convinced that consciousness has no inside: it is nothing but the outward projection toward the world. Intentionality becomes the heart of his ontology.

title: Master and slave in the café
summary: Sartre took Kojève's reading of Hegel — the dialectic of master and slave, of recognition through the gaze — and made it the founding scene of intersubjectivity in Being and Nothingness.

title: A pre-reflective Cartesianism
summary: Sartre kept Descartes's starting point in self-presence but argued that the cogito is itself pre-reflective: consciousness is aware of itself only as it is aware of objects.

title: Existentialism made ethical and concrete
summary: Beauvoir worked Sartrean freedom into an ethics he never quite produced and into the political analysis of women's situation. The Ethics of Ambiguity is the missing companion to Being and Nothingness.

title: Master-slave between the sexes
summary: Beauvoir read the history of women through Hegel's struggle for recognition: woman has been kept in the position of the Other, the inessential, against whom man defines himself as essential.

title: Existence in situation
summary: From Heidegger, Beauvoir kept the notion that human existence is always already thrown into a concrete situation. Gender, she would show, is one of the most decisive such situations.

title: The long line of feminist reason
summary: The Second Sex takes its place in a tradition Wollstonecraft began: insisting that the Enlightenment's universal rationality applies, without exception, to women — and demanding the conditions that would make that real.

title: The lived body in phenomenology
summary: Merleau-Ponty pursued Husserl's late hints about the lifeworld and made the body its center. Perception is not a mental episode added to a brain; it is the body's pre-reflective grip on the world.

title: Embodied being-in-the-world
summary: Where Heidegger spoke of Dasein's being-in-the-world, Merleau-Ponty filled in the body that Heidegger had left strangely abstract. We are in the world because we have hands, eyes, and a posture.

title: Against the empty for-itself
summary: Merleau-Ponty criticized Sartre's picture of consciousness as a transparent for-itself opposed to a brute in-itself. We are neither pure freedom nor inert thing; we are embodied perspectives in commerce with a world.

title: How to live after the death of God
summary: Camus inherited from Nietzsche the question: once the metaphysical horizon goes, how does one affirm life without lying to oneself? The Myth of Sisyphus is one extended answer.

title: Refusing the leap
summary: Camus admired Kierkegaard's diagnosis of the absurd but refused his cure. Faith, for Camus, is a philosophical suicide; the absurd has to be held open, not resolved.

title: Ethics before being
summary: Levinas accepted Heidegger as one of the great philosophers of the century but reproached his ontology for forgetting the face of the Other. Before any inquiry into being, we are already addressed.

title: Phenomenology in the service of ethics
summary: Levinas wrote his doctoral thesis on Husserl. Phenomenological description of how the Other appears becomes the method by which ethics displaces ontology.

title: The Cartesian infinity reawakened
summary: Levinas revived a moment in the Meditations that Descartes used to prove God: the idea of the infinite in a finite mind. He read it as the formal structure of every encounter with the Other.

title: Frege rescued — and Russell's paradox
summary: Russell discovered Frege when almost no one else had and made his logicism the starting point of analytic philosophy. He also discovered the paradox that brought Frege's system down.

title: Russell's revolt
summary: Russell and Moore began as Hegelians and turned violently against the British idealism of their teachers. The analytic tradition begins as a polemic against a Hegel they may not always have understood.

title: Leibniz read as the prisoner of his logic
summary: Russell argued in A Critical Exposition that Leibniz's whole metaphysics depended on assuming a subject-predicate form of judgment. Drop the assumption and the system loses its foundation.

title: The Humean problem persists
summary: Russell granted Hume that induction has no purely logical justification. The Problems of Philosophy lays out the difficulty with admirable clarity and few illusions about a solution.

title: From co-author to process metaphysics
summary: Whitehead spent a decade with Russell building Principia and then walked away from logic into speculative metaphysics. Process and Reality is his answer to the question Russell never asked: what is the world such that mathematics can describe it?

title: Refutation by lifted hand
summary: Moore's strategy against idealism was philosophical jiu-jitsu: any premise leading to the denial of his hand is less certain than the hand. Common sense breaks the spell of British Hegelianism.

title: The naturalistic fallacy
summary: Moore charged Bentham (and Mill) with defining 'good' as a natural property like pleasure. Goodness, he replied, is simple and indefinable; any such definition leaves the question 'but is pleasure good?' open.

title: Pupil who outgrew the teacher
summary: Wittgenstein arrived in Cambridge as Russell's most demanding student. The Tractatus accepts Russell's logical atomism and then drives it past where Russell was willing to go.

title: Through Frege, into logic
summary: Wittgenstein read Frege before Russell and remained marked by him. Many of the Tractatus's foundational distinctions — sense, reference, assertion, function — are Fregean in origin.

title: Schopenhauer in the last propositions
summary: The famous closing pages of the Tractatus — about ethics, the mystical, what cannot be said — come from a teenager's reading of Schopenhauer that Wittgenstein never abandoned.

title: Wittgenstein against Wittgenstein
summary: The Investigations argue, page by page, against the Tractatus: meaning is not a picture but a use; language is not a single calculus but a family of games; the philosopher's task is therapy, not theory.

quotes.text: For a large class of cases of the use of the word 'meaning' — though not for all — this word can be explained in this way: the meaning of a word is its use in the language.

title: Tractatus into Vienna Circle
summary: The Vienna Circle read the Tractatus aloud. Carnap took its constraints on meaningful language as a foundation for the verificationism Wittgenstein himself eventually rejected.

title: Aufbau as Russellian program
summary: Carnap's Logical Structure of the World tries to carry out Russell's program of constructing the empirical world from elementary experiences using only the resources of formal logic.

title: Fregean logic as philosophy's instrument
summary: Carnap studied with Frege and built a career out of treating philosophical problems as questions about the choice of linguistic framework — Frege's logic in the service of dissolving metaphysics.

title: Two dogmas dismantled
summary: Quine, Carnap's friend and inheritor, attacked the very distinction Carnap's project rested on. Once the line between analytic and synthetic is blurred, no purely linguistic philosophy can survive.

title: Quine without language games
summary: Quine shared Wittgenstein's hostility to mental meanings but reached the conclusion behaviorally: there is no fact of the matter about translation beyond patterns of stimulus and response.

title: Hume completed by science
summary: Quine declared himself a Humean: empiricist, naturalist, skeptical of necessary truths. But where Hume despaired, Quine recommended doing epistemology as a chapter of empirical psychology.

title: Kripkenstein
summary: Kripke reconstructed an argument from the Investigations into the famous rule-following paradox. The result — that no fact about an individual fixes what a rule means — has been argued about ever since under the nickname Kripkenstein.

title: Against descriptivism
summary: Russell had treated names as disguised descriptions. Kripke argued in Naming and Necessity that names are rigid designators picked up in a causal chain — and that this changes what is necessary and what is contingent.

title: Radical interpretation after radical translation
summary: Davidson took Quine's setup — the field linguist confronting an unknown language — and asked what it would take to interpret another at all. The answer involves a principle of charity Quine resisted.

title: Wittgensteinian themes formalized
summary: Davidson's holism about meaning, his attention to how interpretation actually works, and his suspicion of inner mental representations all share family resemblance with the later Wittgenstein.

title: Inheriting and complicating Quine
summary: Putnam began as a Quinean naturalist and never lost the conviction that science matters for philosophy. But the externalism in 'The Meaning of Meaning' eventually pushed him beyond Quine in ways neither could have predicted.

title: Late Putnam reads late Wittgenstein
summary: In his later years Putnam moved away from scientific realism toward something close to Wittgenstein: meaning is normative, embedded in practice, not derivable from physics.

title: Kantian normativity without things in themselves
summary: Sellars treated empirical knowledge as a normative achievement, the way Kant treated synthesis. The 'Myth of the Given' is an attack on the empiricist counterpart of the Kantian thing in itself.

title: Hegelian inferentialism
summary: Sellars famously called Hegel his great hero. The space of reasons, in which concepts are constituted by inferential roles, is a Hegelian thought transposed into analytic philosophy.

title: Marxism against the locomotive
summary: Benjamin kept Marx's commitment to revolution but rejected the picture of history as inexorable progress. His angel of history is blown forward by the storm of accumulating ruins.

title: Marx as critical method
summary: Horkheimer defined critical theory against traditional theory: not contemplation of social facts but practical analysis that aims to change the conditions of its own possibility. The lineage is Marxian.

title: Dialectic without the Absolute
summary: Horkheimer kept Hegelian dialectic as a way of thinking contradictions inside social formations while rejecting any teleology that would guarantee their reconciliation.

title: Dialectic that refuses to close
summary: Hegel's dialectic ends with reconciliation. Adorno kept the movement but cut off the resolution: negative dialectics insists on the non-identical, the remainder that no concept fully captures.

title: Commodity analysis into the cultural sphere
summary: Adorno carried Marx's analysis of the commodity form into music, film, and intellectual life. The culture industry is not an opinion about taste but a structural diagnosis.

title: Kant's autonomous art under suspicion
summary: Adorno admired Kant's analysis of aesthetic autonomy but historicized it. Art's promise of freedom from instrumental reason is shadowed by complicity with the social order that protects it.

title: Critique with Nietzschean nerve
summary: Adorno took from Nietzsche the willingness to suspect ostensibly noble ideals — truth, morality, art — without losing the dialectical equipment to analyze what they conceal.

title: Reading Benjamin's debt and disagreement
summary: Adorno owed Benjamin the idea of the constellation — thought arranged so that the configuration, not the concept, illuminates. He also fought with him over the readability of mass culture.

title: Marx for the postwar West
summary: Marcuse held to Marx's diagnostic intent — capitalism produces what it then satisfies — but argued that the working class no longer played the revolutionary role Marx had assigned it.

title: Hegelian negativity recharged
summary: Reason and Revolution defended Hegel as the great philosopher of negative thought against fascist appropriations and bourgeois pieties alike. The dialectic, properly handled, is critical.

title: Heidegger as point of departure
summary: Marcuse studied with Heidegger before breaking with him over Nazism. His early work tried to combine Heideggerian existence with Marxist concreteness — a project the master himself made impossible.

title: The young Marx into social psychology
summary: Fromm read the early Marx — manuscripts on alienated labor — as a humanism that could be married to psychoanalysis. The result was a social psychology of unfreedom.

title: Out of the Dialectic of Enlightenment
summary: Habermas accepted Adorno's diagnosis of instrumental reason but refused his despair. Communicative rationality, embedded in everyday language, keeps a normative resource the Frankfurt elders had given up.

title: Kantian universality in discourse
summary: Habermas rewrote the Kantian categorical imperative as a procedural requirement: a norm is justified if all those affected could accept it in unforced discussion.

title: Marx as theory of social interests
summary: In Knowledge and Human Interests, Habermas read Marx as the discoverer of the social-interest dimension of knowledge. He then placed Marxian critique alongside, not above, hermeneutic and empirical-analytic inquiry.

title: Hegel's spirit brought down to earth
summary: Dilthey kept Hegel's intuition that mind is essentially historical but refused his confidence that history follows a logic. The human sciences must describe lived experience, not derive it from the Idea.

title: Truth and Method after Being and Time
summary: Gadamer studied with Heidegger and turned his hints about understanding into a full hermeneutics. Interpretation is not a method we apply but the way we are in the world.

title: Against hermeneutical method
summary: Dilthey wanted a method for the human sciences. Gadamer argued that understanding is not a method at all; it happens in conversation with tradition, whose prejudices are conditions, not obstacles.

title: Phenomenology as long detour
summary: Ricoeur translated and absorbed Husserl. He kept phenomenology as starting point but argued that the self appears to itself only through the symbols, narratives, and works in which it is sedimented.

title: Hermeneutics with epistemology
summary: Ricoeur followed Heidegger's hermeneutic turn but refused to leave behind the questions of method and validation that Heidegger had dismissed as too scientific.

title: Hermeneutics with text and distance
summary: Ricoeur extended Gadamer's hermeneutics to written texts. The distance writing puts between text and reader is not an obstacle but the condition of interpretation.

title: Reading the masters of suspicion
summary: Ricoeur grouped Nietzsche with Marx and Freud as masters of suspicion: each taught us to read consciousness as a surface concealing forces. Hermeneutics must pass through that lesson without ending in it.

title: Structuralism leaves linguistics
summary: Lévi-Strauss transposed Saussure's structural method from language to kinship and myth. The variables differ; the search for the underlying combinatorial system does not.

title: Rousseau as the first anthropologist
summary: Lévi-Strauss called Rousseau the founder of the human sciences. Tristes Tropiques is Rousseauian in mood as well as method: a sober elegy for the modes of life modernity destroys.

title: The unconscious structured like a language
summary: Lacan rewrote Freud through Saussure. The unconscious is not a reservoir of urges but a chain of signifiers, slipping past their signifieds — a linguistic structure rather than a biological depth.

title: Hegel via Kojève
summary: Lacan attended Kojève's seminars on the Phenomenology and absorbed the doctrine that desire is desire for the desire of the Other. The mirror stage and his theory of recognition descend from that.

title: Borrowing from the master of being
summary: Lacan read Heidegger appreciatively. The idea that speech makes us who we are — that we are spoken before we speak — owes much to Heidegger's reflections on language.

title: From genealogy of morals to genealogy of power
summary: Foucault inherited Nietzsche's genealogical method and applied it to prisons, clinics, sexuality, and madness. Each apparently natural category turns out to have a history of struggle behind it.

title: Power without the class subject
summary: Foucault accepted that power is fundamental but rejected the Marxist concentration of it in class and state. Power circulates capillary-fashion through everyday techniques — discipline, examination, confession.

title: Genealogies of the present
summary: Foucault late in life acknowledged Heidegger's decisive importance: the history of how being has been thought becomes, in Foucault's hands, the history of how the modern subject has been made.

title: Critique as historical attitude
summary: Foucault's late essay on Kant's 'What is Enlightenment?' redefines critique. It is not the search for transcendental limits but the historical practice of asking how we came to be what we are.

title: After the death of man
summary: Foucault's early structuralism declared the figure of 'man' — the subject of phenomenology — a recent invention soon to be erased. Husserl's transcendental ego becomes a historical formation.

title: Carrying Heidegger past himself
summary: Deconstruction is a translation of Heidegger's Destruktion of metaphysics, with one twist: Derrida finds Heidegger himself still inside the metaphysics of presence he claimed to overcome.

title: Phonocentrism unraveled
summary: Derrida's first book argues that Husserl's phenomenology, despite itself, privileges the voice as the bearer of self-presence. Once writing is allowed back in, the whole structure trembles.

title: Différance after Saussure
summary: Saussure said signs work by difference. Derrida agreed and pressed further: the differences never come to rest in a final signified. Différance is Saussurean difference set permanently in motion.

title: Nietzsche as writer to be read
summary: Derrida read Nietzsche as exemplary precisely because his style refuses the philosophical demand for a single voice. Reading him deconstructively means letting him have several.

title: Plato's pharmacy
summary: Derrida read the Phaedrus to show how Plato condemned writing as a pharmakon — both poison and cure. The ambivalence runs through the whole text and undoes the hierarchy of speech over writing.

title: Violence and metaphysics
summary: In a long essay, Derrida both honored and questioned Levinas: ethics first is a beautiful thought, but it speaks the language of metaphysics in trying to escape it.

title: Nietzsche as philosopher of difference
summary: Deleuze read Nietzsche against Hegel: difference is not the negation of identity but the affirmative force out of which identities are made. Nietzsche becomes the master of an ethics of yes.

title: Spinoza as the prince of philosophers
summary: Deleuze called Spinoza the prince of philosophers and rewrote his Ethics as a metaphysics of pure immanence. Substance is replaced by a plane on which singularities and affects move.

title: Bergsonism revived
summary: Deleuze pulled Bergson out of the postwar shadow and made his duration central to a metaphysics of difference. Time itself differs from itself; the past coexists with the present.

title: An empiricism of relations
summary: Deleuze's first book read Hume as the founder of a transcendental empiricism: subjects are habits, identities are bundles, and relations are not in the terms but outside them.

title: Leibniz reread through the Baroque
summary: Deleuze rewrote Leibniz as a thinker of folds: a monad is a perspective folded into the universe and the universe folded into a monad. A Baroque metaphysics of curvature.

title: Kantian sublime as postmodern figure
summary: Lyotard located in Kant's analysis of the sublime — the failure of imagination to present what reason demands — the philosophical structure of the postmodern condition.

title: Wittgensteinian phrases of justice
summary: Lyotard rewrote politics in Wittgensteinian terms: not a single discourse but a plurality of phrase regimes, between which there is no shared rule but only the differend.

title: Beyond the critique of political economy
summary: Baudrillard accused Marx of staying inside the productivist logic he wanted to critique. Late capitalism is no longer about use values but about signs that signify status, identity, and other signs.

title: Signs that cut loose from things
summary: Saussure pointed out that signs work by their differences from other signs. Baudrillard pushed the thought to its extreme: in late media societies, signs no longer answer to anything outside their own circulation.

title: Arendt reads the third Critique politically
summary: Arendt rejected the moral universalism of Kant's second Critique and turned to the third. The reflective judgment of taste — judging the particular without subsuming it under a rule — became her model for political judgment.

title: Plurality against the solitary Dasein
summary: Arendt was Heidegger's student and lover before she was his exile. Her later work corrects him: there is no Dasein in the singular. We exist in plurality, and politics is the disclosure of that plurality.

title: Aristotelian action recovered
summary: Arendt rescued from Aristotle the distinction between making and acting, between poiesis and praxis. The dignity of action — speech and deed in public — is the heart of her political philosophy.

title: Augustinian beginning as political fact
summary: Arendt's doctoral dissertation was on Augustine's concept of love. From him she took the thought that every birth is a beginning — natality — and made it the metaphysical ground of political action.

title: Against the labor-society
summary: Arendt admired Marx but accused him of confusing labor with work and of glorifying both. The modern reduction of action to labor, she argued, eliminates exactly what politics needs.

title: Kantian autonomy made institutional
summary: Rawls described his theory as broadly Kantian. The veil of ignorance is a procedural device for picking principles a free, equal, autonomous chooser could accept — Kant's universalizability put into a thought experiment.

title: Contract theory revived
summary: Rawls explicitly placed himself in the social contract tradition of Locke and Kant. The veil of ignorance is a contemporary reformulation of the conditions under which consent to principles is rational.

title: Hobbes's contract refined
summary: Rawls inherited Hobbes's general device — agreement on terms under conditions of mutual ignorance — and rebuilt it with the maximin choice rule of his original position.

title: Humean conditions taken seriously
summary: Rawls borrowed from Hume the description of the conditions that make justice both necessary and possible: moderate scarcity, limited altruism, rough equality of vulnerability.

title: Justice as fairness against the sum
summary: Rawls's central target is the utilitarian summing of welfare across persons. A theory of justice cannot, he argued, treat individuals as containers of utility to be added; it must respect the separateness of persons.

title: Wilt Chamberlain against the patterned state
summary: Nozick replied to Rawls that any distributive principle requires continuous interference with voluntary exchanges. The famous Wilt Chamberlain example shows how repeated free choices undo a pattern designed to be just.

title: Lockean entitlements reasserted
summary: Nozick rebuilt political philosophy on Lockean rights of self-ownership and original acquisition. His entitlement theory of justice tracks the history of how holdings came about, not the pattern they form.

title: Recovering the virtues
summary: MacIntyre argued that moral concepts are intelligible only inside a tradition of practices, and that the broken state of modern moral discourse can only be healed by returning to something like Aristotle's account of the virtues.

title: Either Aristotle or Nietzsche
summary: MacIntyre framed modern ethics as a choice: either return to a virtue tradition like Aristotle's, or accept that Nietzsche was right about its impossibility and live in the consequences.

title: From Aristotle to Aquinas
summary: MacIntyre eventually moved beyond Aristotle to Aquinas as the most fully developed example of a tradition-constituted rationality, in which the virtues are grounded in a theology of human flourishing.

title: Hegel for the politics of recognition
summary: Taylor's vast study of Hegel restored him to Anglo-American philosophy and supplied the framework for his later politics of recognition: identities are formed dialogically, not in solitude.

title: Heideggerian background in modern selfhood
summary: Taylor used Heidegger's idea of a pre-theoretical background of understanding to argue that modern selfhood depends on inarticulate sources we usually fail to acknowledge.

title: Wittgenstein in social theory
summary: Taylor's communitarianism leans on Wittgensteinian forms of life: meanings are embedded in shared practices, not in isolated minds — which constrains what liberal political philosophy can plausibly assume.

title: Performativity after The Second Sex
summary: Beauvoir famously said one is not born, but becomes, a woman. Butler radicalized the thought: there is no underlying 'one' who becomes, only the repeated performance through which gender is produced.

title: From discipline to gender
summary: Butler took Foucault's account of the subject as effect of power and applied it to gender. Gender norms do not regulate a pre-existing subject; they help bring it into being.

title: Hegel through Kojève, with feminist eyes
summary: Butler's first book reread the French reception of Hegel — Kojève, Hyppolite — for what it could offer a politics of embodied subjects struggling for recognition.

title: Performativity after Derrida
summary: Butler took Derrida's notion of iteration — that meaning is constituted by repetition that can always go astray — and made it the conceptual heart of gender as performance.

title: Speculum against the phallus
summary: Irigaray studied with Lacan and was expelled from his school for criticizing his account of sexual difference. The symbolic order, she argued, is built around a single masculine pole.

title: Against assimilation to the masculine
summary: Where Beauvoir wanted women to claim the universal hitherto reserved for men, Irigaray feared that this only assimilates women to the masculine. The task is to imagine a feminine that is its own.

title: Antigone against the system
summary: Irigaray reread the figure of Antigone in Hegel to show that the system requires the feminine and then expels her. The dialectic depends on a sacrifice it cannot acknowledge.

title: Beneath the symbolic
summary: Kristeva kept Lacan's symbolic order but added what it represses: the semiotic — bodily rhythms, drives, the maternal voice — that underlies and disrupts symbolic meaning.

title: Negativity as poetic force
summary: Kristeva read Hegelian negativity into language as the force that breaks identity from within. Poetic revolutions of language are local re-enactments of the dialectic's negative moment.

title: Hume's problem and falsification
summary: Popper accepted Hume's verdict that induction can never be justified and asked what science could be on that basis. The answer was falsification: theories are tested by attempts to refute them.

title: Conjectures instead of categories
summary: Popper agreed with Kant that experience requires prior structures but rejected their necessity. We project conjectures onto the world, and the world strikes them down — there is no privileged set.

title: Real problems, not just puzzles
summary: Popper insisted that philosophical problems are real, not pseudo-problems generated by misuses of language — a direct attack on the early Wittgenstein and the Vienna Circle.

title: Against the open conspiracy of history
summary: Popper named Marx among the great enemies of the open society. The doctrine that history follows laws — historicism — generates totalitarian temptations whenever leaders take themselves to know them.

title: Hegel as oracular nuisance
summary: The Open Society devoted long and hostile pages to Hegel, accusing him of replacing arguments with rhetorical gestures and laying the philosophical groundwork for modern totalitarianism.

title: Plato as the great enemy
summary: Popper read the Republic as a blueprint for closed society: ruled by guardians who know best, hostile to change, suspicious of ordinary life. Liberal democracy begins by repudiating it.

title: Science as paradigm shift, not test
summary: Kuhn argued that working scientists do not, as Popper said, try to refute their theories. They solve puzzles within a paradigm until anomalies pile up and the paradigm collapses into a new one.

title: Sophisticated Popperianism
summary: Lakatos defended a Popperian view that absorbed Kuhn's lesson: rationality belongs not to single tests but to research programmes whose hard cores are protected by belts of auxiliary hypotheses.

title: Against irrationalism
summary: Lakatos accused Kuhn of reducing scientific change to mob psychology. Paradigm shifts may be social, but science is not therefore irrational: progressive vs. degenerating programmes can be distinguished.

title: Incommensurability pushed to anarchism
summary: Feyerabend took Kuhn's incommensurability and pushed it further: not only do paradigms differ; no general rules can constrain which one wins. Anything goes — and that is good news for science.

title: Against the methodologist
summary: Feyerabend was Popper's student and turned on him. The history of science, he argued, shows that any rule a methodologist proposes has been violated, at one time or another, by the great discoveries.

title: Mill in the philosophy of science
summary: Feyerabend took Mill's defense of free discussion in On Liberty and applied it to scientific theories. We need a plurality of theories for the same reason we need a plurality of opinions: any monopoly degrades inquiry.
